title: Jīvarāśi Sūtra (The Classes of Living Beings)
summary: The Jīvarāśi Sūtra is a short confession-prayer recited during the daily evening (Devasi) pratikramaṇa. In it the practitioner runs through every category of living being in the universe — counted in the tradition as 84 lakh (8.4 million) wombs or birth-forms (jīva-yoni) — and then asks forgiveness for any creature, in any of those forms, that they have harmed, caused to be harmed, or approved the harming of, whether by thought, word, or deed. It closes with the formula 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' ('may my wrong be undone'). Its purpose is to make the vow of non-violence (ahiṁsā) concrete and exhaustive: by naming all life, no creature is left out of one's repentance.
ritualPlacement: In the Devasi (Daivasika) pratikramaṇa, Jīvarāśi is recited early, in the opening confession cluster: after the Iriyāvahiyam–Tassa Uttari–Annattha kāyotsarga and the Logassa (the second Āvaśyaka), and after Gamaṇāgamaṇo, but before the Aḍhāra Pāpasthānaka (18 sins) and the Dravya-Kṣetra-Kāla-Bhāva declaration that lead into the Karemi Bhante sāmāyika vow. Together Gamaṇāgamaṇo and Jīvarāśi form the practitioner's preliminary self-examination of harm done. As one of the shared core sūtras taught once in the Devasi base (book pp.19–44), the same text is reused per its vidhi in the Rāi pratikramaṇa, and within the extended Pakkhī / Cāumāsī / Sāṁvatsarī sequences, which add their own material but inherit the Devasi core — Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī have no independent sūtra text of their own.
authorAndSource: Anonymous / traditional. The sūtra is a vernacular (classical Gujarati) confession formula of the Śvetāmbara liturgy rather than a canonically authored stotra; no individual author is named in the source book, Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition). Its enumeration of the 84 lakh jīva-yoni rests on standard Jain biological cosmology as systematized in the Tattvārtha Sūtra and allied āgamic and karma-grantha literature.
structureArc: This sūtra is a single continuous verse (v.1) with two movements. First, the enumeration (the bulk of the verse): it counts the birth-forms class by class — 7 lakh each of earth-, water-, fire-, and air-bodied one-sensed beings; 10 lakh individual (pratyeka) and 14 lakh common (sādhāraṇa / nigoda) plant-bodied beings; 2 lakh each of two-, three-, and four-sensed beings; 4 lakh each of celestial (deva), infernal (nāraka), and five-sensed animal (tiryañca-pañcendriya) beings; and 14 lakh humans — summing, by the phrase 'evaṁkāre… chaurāsī lakṣa jīva-yoni,' to the total 84 lakh life-wombs spread across the fourteen rajju-loka ('chaud rāj'). Second, the confession (the closing movement): 'whatever living being I have caused pain to, caused to be pained, or approved the paining of — for all of that, by mind, speech, and body, micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.' The arc thus moves from a complete map of life to an all-encompassing apology to it.
recurringImagery: The governing image is the universe as an exhaustively countable census of life. Two motifs recur and are explained here once: (1) The 84 lakh jīva-yoni — 'yoni' are the distinct kinds of birth-situation a soul may take. Their breakdown by senses is the same ladder of sentience used throughout the corpus: ekendriya (one sense, touch only — the five 'bodies' of earth, water, fire, air, and vegetation), then beïndriya (touch + taste), teïndriya (+ smell), caürindriya (+ sight), and pañcendriya (+ hearing — the deva, nāraka, tiryañca, and human realms). Plant life is split into pratyeka (one soul per body) and sādhāraṇa (innumerable souls sharing one body — the nigoda), the latter holding the largest single share, 14 lakh. (2) The 'chaud rāj loka' (fourteen-rajju cosmos) — the vertical Jain universe through whose whole height these 84 lakh forms are distributed. Naming the classes is itself an act of cultivated awareness: only a creature one can picture is a creature one can resolve never to injure.
keyThemes: Ahiṁsā made exhaustive — repentance that omits no living being
The 84 lakh jīva-yoni as a complete census of life
The ladder of sentience from one-sensed to five-sensed beings
Threefold agency of harm: doing, causing, approving (kṛta–kārita–anumodita)
Micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ as the act of confession and self-correction

glossary.definition: The aggregate or classes of living beings; the title-theme of the sūtra.
A birth-form or womb — a distinct kind of physical situation into which a soul can be born; counted as 84 lakh in total.
Eighty-four lakh (8.4 million) — the traditional total number of jīva-yoni.
One-sensed beings, possessing only touch: the earth-, water-, fire-, air-, and plant-bodied jīvas.
Plant-bodied beings, divided into pratyeka (individual-souled, 10 lakh) and sādhāraṇa (common-bodied / nigoda, 14 lakh).
Common-bodied vegetable life in which innumerable souls share a single body; the most numerous single class (14 lakh).
Two-, three-, and four-sensed beings (2 lakh forms each), adding taste, smell, and sight in turn.
Five-sensed beings possessing hearing as well: the deva, nāraka, tiryañca, and human classes.
Animals (non-human five-sensed beings); 4 lakh forms in this enumeration.
Celestial beings and infernal (hell) beings; 4 lakh forms each in this enumeration.
The fourteen-rajju cosmos — the full vertical extent of the Jain universe across which all jīva-yoni are distributed.
'May my misdeed be in vain / undone' — the standard formula of confession and withdrawal of a transgression.
Pained directly / caused to be pained / approved the paining of — the threefold agency (kṛta–kārita–anumodita) covered by the confession.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this sūtra simply as a roll-call of all life followed by a sincere, all-inclusive apology — let the rising numbers build the felt sense that no creature is too small to matter. An advanced practitioner may meditate on the enumeration as the classical jīva-tattva ladder of sentience, holding mind–speech–body and the kṛta–kārita–anumodita threefold so that the closing micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ becomes a deliberate, fully-scoped ālocanā rather than a rote phrase.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sūtra text, ritual placement within the Devasi pratikramaṇa, and the shared-core sharing model.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti/Umāsvāmī), ch. 2 — classification of jīvas by senses
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal basis for the ekendriya–pañcendriya ladder and the pratyeka/sādhāraṇa (nigoda) division of plant life.

notes: The per-class counts honor the source book's verbatim reading, in which deva, nāraka, and tiryañca-pañcendriya are each 4 lakh and manuṣya is 14 lakh; these sum exactly to 84 lakh (7+7+7+7+10+14+2+2+2+4+4+4+14). A different but widespread enumeration assigns tiryañca-pañcendriya 20 lakh, nāraka 10 lakh, and deva 14 lakh; this is a known traditional variant and is not the reading recited here — the book is treated as the golden source and is not normalized. The provided sūtra text gives 'apkāya' (અપ્કાય); the knowledge base notes this book varies between 'apkāya' and 'apūkāya' elsewhere. The URL for the source book is a stable repository home rather than a verified deep link to this exact edition; treat the printed book itself as the authority.
